content: Sam, 31, backend/ML engineer at a Paris logistics startup. Date 2026-02-20. I need to lock a decision: keep Postgres, add a Redis cache for hot lookups — no database migration. Current problem: some read endpoints (order status, live location lookups, pricing queries) are at 800 read QPS peak, p95 read latency ~250ms, DB CPU spikes during peak routing. Goal: reduce p95 for hot endpoints to <50ms and drop DB CPU by ~30% during peaks. Plan sketch: add Redis as a read-side cache for specific keys only (no schema changes, no moving data out of Postgres). Start with two endpoints: GET /orders/:id/status and GET /pricing?route=... Cache design: keys per order id and per pricing query hash. Target hit rate 80–90%. TTLs: 30s for order status, 5m for pricing. Eviction: volatile-lru; memory budget 4 GB initial. Invalidation: application-level deletes on writes (DEL key when status changes) + TTL fallback; no DB triggers, no DB migration. Rollout: I own implementation, infra will provision a managed Redis (primary+replica), feature-flag the cache, test in staging, then gradual rollout over 3 weeks starting 2026-02-23 and finishing roughly 2026-03-13. Metrics to track: cache hit rate, p95 latency, Postgres CPU, error rate. Cost target: keep infra delta under €250/month. Risks: staleness for some endpoints (acceptable for status/pricing but not for billing), multi-instance invalidation race conditions. Decision now: keep Postgres, add Redis cache for hot lookups — no database migration. I want this written down as the decision and plan so I can send the RFC and task list.
Clear. One sharp question: which endpoints must never be served from the cache (exact paths) so I can mark them as cache-exempt in the plan?

Never cache: POST/PUT/DELETE endpoints that mutate orders or payments; GET /payments/* and any billing-related endpoints (GET /invoices, POST /payments/confirm). Also exclude real-time tracking websocket endpoints that need live data. All reads under /public/stats can be cached. I'll add a short list in the RFC: POST /orders, POST /payments, POST /payments/confirm, GET /payments/*, GET /invoices, websocket /ws/tracking. Still: allow caching for GET /orders/:id/status and GET /pricing only.
Good. For invalidation across app instances: do you prefer application-level DEL on write plus TTL, or adding a lightweight pub/sub channel for cache invalidation messages?

Combine both: application-level DEL on write as primary, and fall back to TTL. For cross-instance safety I'll push a small pub/sub: on every write the service publishes a key-delete message to Redis pub/sub so other instances can evict immediately. Implementation detail: publish only key names, keep message size tiny. No DB triggers, no migrations. Also plan to instrument with a metric 'cache.evictions.via_pubsub' to confirm propagation works.
Understood. Which SLA thresholds and alert rules should I add to the Grafana dashboard for the rollout?

Set alerts: cache hit rate <70% (warning) and <60% (critical); p95 latency for cached endpoints >70ms (warning) and >120ms (critical); Postgres CPU not falling by at least 20% vs baseline after 50% rollout (warning). Also alert if error rate on cached endpoints increases by >0.5%. Wire alerts to pager for SRE on criticals, Slack for warnings. Timeline: enable alerts in staging day 1, production during 20% rollout step.
One tight ops question: managed Redis single-AZ cheaper but riskier — do you want multi-AZ managed Redis with replica and automatic failover (higher cost) given the €250/month cap?

Choose multi-AZ managed Redis with 1 primary + 1 replica for HA; size initially at the smallest plan that provides 4 GB usable memory. If cost exceeds €250/month by more than 20% after quoting, we re-evaluate to a smaller instance and tighter TTLs. Persistence: enable RDB snapshots only, disable AOF to keep write amplification low. Eviction policy volatile-lru. Backup once a day. Final decision summary: keep Postgres, add managed Redis for hot lookups, application-level DEL + pub/sub + TTL invalidation, TTLs 30s/5m, 4 GB budget, multi-AZ replica, feature-flagged rollout 2026-02-23 to 2026-03-13, metrics and alerts as listed, no database migration.
Got it. Final quick check: who signs off and who runs the production rollout?
